sentence_reviews.text: Explicit entities, relations, types, and constraints represent structured world knowledge.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA knowledge representation material; Koller/Friedman graphical models background for structured relational representations.
sentence_reviews.notes: Entities, relations, types, and constraints are standard knowledge-representation elements. The exact wording is a synthesis.

sentence_reviews.text: The role of Knowledge graphs, ontologies & schemas is to connect Graphs, hypergraphs & relational data, Databases & information retrieval to downstream capabilities including Neuro-symbolic & physics-informed learning, Expert systems, production rules & rule engines, Neuro-symbolic models.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA knowledge representation and expert systems content; DDIA relational/database context; graph-network reference for graph-structured data.
sentence_reviews.notes: The sources support knowledge representation, graph structures, and database context, but the downstream neuro-symbolic/physics-informed list is not directly supported by the cited packet references.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA supports symbolic knowledge representation generally, but not the full neuro-symbolic and physics-informed capability label.
sentence_reviews.notes: Knowledge graphs can support neuro-symbolic systems, but the cited evidence does not directly support physics-informed learning.

sentence_reviews.text: substrate for Expert systems, production rules & rule engines
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA knowledge-based agents and knowledge representation material.
sentence_reviews.notes: Structured symbolic knowledge is central to expert systems and rule-based reasoning, although 'substrate for' is atlas wording.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA supports symbolic knowledge representation; the packet references do not directly discuss neuro-symbolic models.
sentence_reviews.notes: The symbolic side is supported, but the neuro-symbolic model claim would need a more specific source.

sentence_reviews.text: Encoding choices can discard information, amplify bias, or make important invariances difficult to learn. Its direct dependencies include Graphs, hypergraphs & relational data, Databases & information retrieval.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA and DDIA are relevant to knowledge representation and databases, but do not directly state this generic limitation.
sentence_reviews.notes: The limitations sentence appears generic and is not supported exactly by the cited sources.

sentence_reviews.text: Knowledge graphs, ontologies & schemas consumes, emits, or organizes information using Graphs, hypergraphs & relational data.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA knowledge representation; graph-network reference for graph-structured relational data.
sentence_reviews.notes: Knowledge graphs are graph/relational structures. Hypergraphs are broader than directly evidenced but compatible with the dependency label.

sentence_reviews.text: Knowledge graphs, ontologies & schemas rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Databases & information retrieval.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: DDIA database systems context; AIMA knowledge representation and retrieval-related AI context.
sentence_reviews.notes: Databases are relevant for storage/querying, but knowledge graphs do not universally require database/IR capabilities in the narrow sense.

sentence_reviews.text: Knowledge graphs, ontologies & schemas rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Discrete mathematics & logic.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA logic and knowledge representation material.
sentence_reviews.notes: Ontologies and logical constraints depend on discrete/logical formalisms; wording is graph-derived.

edge_reviews.notes: Knowledge graphs are graph/relational representations. The represents_with/core edge is defensible.
Database and retrieval systems commonly support knowledge graphs, but the core requires relation is too strong for all ontologies/schemas.
Logic is a common foundation for ontologies and constraints, but not every knowledge graph requires the full dependency.
